question_text: A syringe contains air at room temperature. The plunger is pushed in, reducing the volume to half. What happens to the pressure inside the syringe?

options.label: A
options.text: The pressure stays the same because the temperature hasn't changed

options.label: B
options.text: The pressure doubles because particles are forced into half the space and collide more often with the walls

options.label: C
options.text: The pressure halves because there is less air

options.label: D
options.text: The pressure increases slightly but not to double

explanation: When volume decreases, particles are packed more closely together. They collide more frequently with the syringe walls, causing pressure to increase. If volume is halved, pressure doubles (assuming temperature stays constant).
